author: @josephpk4878
text:
I was thinking this was a good idea, until I realized that it was being used to blow air ONTO the turbine, rather than INTO the turbine. If it were blown into the turbine (using some form of wind collector and tube/ventura system) then the exhaust from the turbine would be using the shroud blades as a "launch pad" to increase RMPs. When the wind is collected by the shroud blades and focused on the turbine, it may appear that it's causing the turbine to accelerate on its own, but I believe that the reason for it working better in low wind speeds is that you've simply increased the total surface area and air volume of the collector. Should you just remove the shroud and use a larger turbine, the effect would probably be comparable.

I'd like to see this same setup, but with the air being forced into the center of the turbine. Rebuild the shroud with flat blades that are aligned perpendicular to the angle of the turbine blades, which would create a flat surface for the air to push against... and that's how I believe you'll see higher RPMs, with the same volume of air.

I use a palm plane for this... usually.

author: @PaddyNinja
text: I've heard the Shroud also called a Vane.  The Vane basically directs the wind onto the blades causing rotation, and blocks the fins on the opposite side of the turbine from the same directional wind which would slow it down or cause a "drag" effect.  So in essence it promotes acceleration and prevents braking.  The Vanes are normally straight though, but whatever works, it's all trial and error as Mr Smith points out.  And the bonus is it supports your turbine.  Nice one Mr. Smith : )

author: @travismoore7849
text: This may be beyond insane. But take your MHD magnet core of two attracted doughnut magnets. Then the electrodes are two parallel rings on the out side of the magnet core. Paint Radium Chloride on the cathode. Then put in a cone to funnel wind though the cold cathode mhd. If it works the ionization should put a voltage and current on parallel rings of the device as the wind blows without any moving parts.

author: @williamarmstrong7199
text:
Plans to make wind generators like this were published in books produced by CAT the Center for Alternative Technology, in Mid Wales back in the 1970's . https://g.co/kgs/RKofnV

Recently they have lost their way a bit and having started the wind , solar and water power revolution have now been overtaken by industry and the original visionaries retired (or left to make £millions setting up the 1st wind power companies and gas generation from waste in digesters).
They still do stirling work on teaching sustainable living, waterless loo's  and converting things like 200 litre oil drums to act as water pumps, for 3rd world solutions to problems.
Well worth a visit.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
Might I suggest, if you can find a place [perhaps close to the ocean] and/or on an elevated structure, so that you can rise your machine up above the ground clutter [which is killing off most of the usable wind, just normal nature to do so] perhaps you can find a sufficiently strong wind which is sufficiently steady that you can perhaps obtain a realistic amount of power from the machine.
Also, if the torque is not adequate, but the wind speed is reasonably high, one can create the equivalent effect of a high speed generator simply by dividing the poles more.
I did this once, I replaced a wheel with 4 very large, very strong magnets over a single coil with a wheel with 48 smaller [still relatively strong magnets] and 12 coils [ideally I would have had 24 coils, but at the time, I was using scavenged coils from little AC to DC power adapters like you would use to plug into a small device and charge it, but now I have a coil winder and very fine gauge magnet wire, so should I ever revisit this (and since I have a 5 foot diameter 6 blade fan my brothers think I definitely should) I shall indeed make more coils, [however, I will do my best to space them on a 4:3 or else 7:3 ratio so as to avoid the whole 'cogging' issue, even though this requires more materials, because cogging is something that needlessly saps power from a wind generator and many large ones are made from the factory to be totally non-cogging, despite the extra cost, because it allows them to operate at far lower power inputs and lower wind speeds]. ~( 'w')/

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: IRC they are high resistance and hard to spin under light torque that an average ground breeze would create aka it would not spin most of the time.

replies.author: @daviddouglas4200
replies.text: @Barskor1 8 Ohms-- high resistance ?  ANY generator when "on load" is hard to spin , just depends on the load-- dont get somthing for nothing.....

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: @daviddouglas4200 Internal gearing but whatever... this is why produced energy goes to a storage system with the highest capacity possible so you have a limited load/resistance.

author: @shannonr6976
text:
Hey I seen this leaf caught in a spider's web
In such a way that it was dangling from its stem . So it didn't matter how hard or erratic it was it still was spinning.  I always wanted to try to replicate that. Its really easy to replicate what I'm talking about.  Just grab a leaf and stick it to a spider's web.  I can see a motor hanging with a flexible poles and a leaf shape to  drive it. Its one of a kind.  Please help me out if it's a success . I can really use it.

replies.author: @midnightgardener8346
replies.text: I’ve seen this quite a few times now. Mesmerizing to watch. I’ve seen spiders float leaves like this to build webs too. I bet he could easily do it with that speaker design and some weighted cordage of sorts

replies.author: @shannonr6976
replies.text: The weight of the motor is a issue.  Some how if you can extend off the motor generator upward.  As to balance it to float a long with a flexible shaft of some kind . It might work. And I love the idea of the speaker why not incorporate that to.

author: @coldfinger459sub0
text: Actually you need an airfoil to scoop the win to a certain percentage in portion of the squirrel cage blower wheel. Then you need to dedicate a straight airflow exiting low pressure zone a certain percentage for the air to leave in a different direction.              You almost got the concept down and a slight understanding but you’re missing part of the big picture HVAC and airflow just happen to be my profession.  And we actually work with those kind of blower wheels

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: Add holes to the bottom and top over the blower wheel? Edit I think Robert was going for an omnidirectional setup so a rudder and a pivot point to turn it into the wind would not be needed.
